title: Apps targeted at US troops contain Chinese and Russian code
gist: A study of over 220 apps marketed to US troops found more than 1 in 8 carry embedded code from Chinese, Russian, or other foreign firms, including Huawei's HMS Core and Russia's Yandex, and US Central Command has already confirmed adversaries used commercial location data to target personnel near the Iran standoff in the Middle East.

themes.theme: Foreign code embedded in military-marketed apps

insights.claim: Researchers from Purdue University, the US Military Academy at West Point, and Florida International University examined more than 220 mobile apps marketed toward US military personnel and found more than one in eight contained software built by companies in China, Russia, or other foreign nations.
insights.speaker: WIRED
entities: US Military Academy at West Point
insights.so_what: Establishes the scale of the exposure: a meaningful share of apps troops actually use carry code from countries the Pentagon considers adversaries.

insights.claim: A popular app used by service members to rate living conditions on their own bases includes code from Huawei, the Chinese telecom flagged by US regulators as a national security threat in 2020, and two other apps built by Russian companies incorporate the Russian ad service Yandex.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: A base living-conditions app is an unusually sensitive vector since it could reveal specific base locations and conditions to foreign-linked code.

insights.claim: Sixty-four percent of the examined apps contained third-party SDKs, typically used for analytics and advertising, that can track user behavior including location and share that data with outside companies; 76 distinct SDKs were found in total, tracing to China, Russia, Israel, India, Germany, and other countries, with about 7 percent of apps carrying code from a nation the Pentagon considers adversarial.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Shows the exposure isn't limited to a handful of apps but is systemic across the third-party ad/analytics supply chain feeding military-marketed apps.

insights.claim: Forty percent of the apps studied collected or shared more data than they disclosed in their Google or Apple app store listings.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Indicates app store privacy disclosures are unreliable for a large share of apps troops might trust based on store labeling alone.

insights.claim: Twelve apps contained Huawei's HMS Core software kit, which advertises the ability to map user locations, deliver ads, and store images and video; several of these apps were built for state National Guard organizations, and in at least one case the Huawei code was smuggled in as a dependency in a commercial notification tool without the app developer's own knowledge.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Shows foreign-code exposure can enter an app invisibly through a third-party dependency, meaning even a well-intentioned developer may not know their app is at risk.

insights.claim: Researchers observed no data currently flowing to Huawei's servers from the studied apps, but note that an SDK can be updated remotely at any time, meaning code that is dormant today could become spyware tomorrow.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Reframes the risk as latent rather than active: absence of current data exfiltration is not proof of future safety, since remote SDK updates can change behavior without a new app release.
insights.quote: Code that is dormant today can still be spyware tomorrow.

themes.theme: Real-world stakes: troops already being targeted

insights.claim: In April, US Central Command told Senator Ron Wyden it had received multiple threat reports of adversaries exploiting commercial location data to target or surveil American personnel in the Middle East, where US forces remain in a standoff with the Iranian military over the Strait of Hormuz — the first official confirmation that troops in an active war zone were being tracked through the data-broker economy.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Elevates this from a theoretical privacy concern to a confirmed operational security threat against troops in an active conflict zone.

insights.claim: Prior WIRED investigations have shown location data harvested from ordinary apps tracing US service members to their homes, their children's schools, and off-base establishments where troops are prohibited from being seen, which experts warn could help foreign spies identify personnel with access to sensitive sites or map when a facility is least guarded.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Establishes the concrete mechanism by which commercial ad-tech location data becomes a national-security risk, not just a personal-privacy one.

insights.claim: Neither Google Play's Data Safety section nor Apple's App Store Privacy Labels disclose the country of origin of the third-party software running inside an app, leaving users with no straightforward way to identify foreign-code exposure before installing.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Identifies a specific, fixable transparency gap in existing app store disclosure frameworks that currently leaves military users unable to self-protect.

insights.claim: A survey of 103 military-affiliated Americans found more than 83 percent used at least one app engaging in data practices they found uncomfortable, averaging more than three such apps per participant, and 76 to 83 percent said they were extremely uncomfortable with apps containing code from China, Russia, Iran, or North Korea.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Shows a wide gap between service members' stated discomfort with foreign-code exposure and their actual (often unknowing) app usage.

insights.claim: Survey participants reported being more comfortable with data collection when an app was specifically branded for military use.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: A military-branded app may paradoxically lower users' guard even though branding provides no actual security guarantee, making troops more vulnerable to exactly the apps examined in this study.

insights.claim: Nearly two-thirds of surveyed military-affiliated participants said they received little or no institutional guidance on personal app use, and of those who did receive some guidance, nearly three-quarters called it inadequate.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Points to an institutional gap the Pentagon could close with better guidance, given it declined to comment for the story.

insights.claim: Participants ranked in-phone warnings that alert users when an installed app contains foreign or unknown third-party code as both the most effective and the most likely mitigation they would support, ahead of federal data-broker restrictions, independent privacy-disclosure audits, and stricter bans on foreign code in military-marketed apps, which drew nearly identical levels of support to each other.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Suggests a lightweight, on-device warning system could be the most actionable and well-received near-term policy fix compared to broader legislative or regulatory approaches.
